steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question tests punctuation conventions. The left side of the blank ("The number of jams available for tasting varied") is a complete independent clause. The right side ("some shoppers had twenty-four different options, others only six") elaborates on that variation. The blank must introduce that elaboration in a way that is grammatically correct.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• "varied:": A colon after an independent clause correctly introduces a list or elaboration. Here it sets up the explanation of how the number varied, which is its conventional use. ✓
  • "varied,": A comma alone joining two independent clauses creates a comma splice; no coordinating conjunction follows the comma to make the connection grammatical. ✗
  • "varied, while": The subordinating conjunction "while" signals contrast or simultaneity, but the clause that follows is not a contrasting or simultaneous event — it simply describes the variation. This produces an illogical sentence. ✗
  • "varied while": Same logical problem as C: "while" implies the variation in jam quantity happened at the same time as some shoppers having twenty-four options, but what follows is a description of the variation itself, not a separate simultaneous occurrence. ✗
That matches the correct answer — A is correct.
